Executive Summary · March 2026

Community Health Profile

A portrait of health, service access, and community resilience in the English-speaking community of the Magdalen Islands.

Prepared By
Helena Burke
Les Entreprises HMR Inc.
Commissioned By
Council for Anglophone
Magdalen Islanders (CAMI)
Supported By
CHSSN · Health Canada
Networking & Partnership Initiative
The Community at a Glance

665 people. One community.
An uncertain future.

The English-speaking community of the Magdalen Islands is small, geographically distinct, and navigating a complex web of demographic, economic, and service-access pressures. This brief distils the findings of the 2026 Community Health Profile — the most comprehensive portrait of this community ever compiled.

665
English speakers on the islands
5.3% of the total island population — well below the provincial average of 14.9%.
125+
Community voices captured
Parents, youth, adults, seniors, school professionals, and institutional partners.
23–40%
Delayed or avoided care
Across every age group, because services were not available in English.
97%
Seniors wish to age in place
But most have no plan and inadequate local supports in English.

Based on the 2021 Census, community-wide surveys completed in 2026, key informant interviews, a review of commissioned reports since 2018, and CHSSN data.

CAMI · Community Health Profile — Executive Summary02
The Landscape

A community of strength — and structural strain.

Local culture and small-business presence remain central to the community's identity and social fabric.

The English-speaking community of the Magdalen Islands is ageing. Seniors (65+) now represent 21.8% of the population, while children (0–14) account for only 12.8%. Long-term decline has persisted since the 1970s, driven primarily by youth outmigration for education and opportunity.

Economic conditions are paradoxical. The lobster fishery and tourism deliver strong seasonal incomes, but unemployment among English speakers sits at 25.5% — more than double the French-speaking rate.

Despite these pressures, social cohesion remains high. Tight-knit networks, community pride, active cultural life, and a strong diaspora form the community's enduring strengths — alongside CAMI as a central organising anchor.

But language is a persistent barrier to health. Between 23% and 40% of respondents in every age group have delayed or avoided seeking help because services were not available in English.

The core paradox

A community that is economically strong yet socially and demographically declining. High peak incomes do not translate into year-round stability, and seasonal employment shapes wellbeing, social patterns, and service access in ways provincial programs fail to address.

CAMI · Community Health Profile — Executive Summary03
Cross-Cutting Finding · 01

Mental health is a community-wide concern.

Stress, anxiety, and social isolation emerge in every survey group without exception — from children to seniors. The pattern is not age-specific. It is systemic.

YMHI Youth Club — a safe, non-judgemental space for youth 12–17.

Youth

88% self-rate their mental health as good, yet 71% feel stressed or overwhelmed often. Only 4 of 17 are fully comfortable talking to someone when struggling. Six do not know where to go for support.

Adults

83% have experienced stress at least sometimes in the past six months; 36% experience it often. Finances and health concerns lead the list of stressors.

Seniors at a Quilt Club gathering — community connection as protective factor.

Seniors

22 of 34 seniors lack companionship at least some of the time. 20 of 34 do not participate in community activities regularly. Loneliness is widespread despite most having family nearby.

Children & Parents

School professionals report a sustained five-year decline in student wellbeing. Every parent surveyed feels stressed as a parent; 8 of 20 feel this way often.
"Mental health is not primarily an individual issue. It is a structural one — rooted in isolation, economic precarity, service gaps, and the social impacts of living in a small, remote, minority-language community."
— From the Cross-Cutting Analysis
CAMI · Community Health Profile — Executive Summary04
Cross-Cutting Finding · 02

Language remains a structural health barrier.

Health Fair — bringing services into community spaces is one strategy for reaching English-speaking residents.

English is offered at the front door but rarely deep in the service system. Of nine francophone partner organisations surveyed, none rated their English-language capacity as strong or full. Five described it as moderate with limitations; four as limited to occasional availability.

Bilingual staffing is the proximate cause. This constraint was cited seven times by partners — far exceeding all other factors. Recruitment of bilingual professionals is difficult for most organisations.

Service navigability is a systemic failure. When asked whether anglophone clients easily understand how to access their services, only 1 of 9 said yes — 5 said they were uncertain. Even where English-language services exist, friction is enough to make them effectively inaccessible.

The impact on adults is especially sharp. The two largest barriers — long wait times and not having a family doctor — were cited by 21 respondents each. Services not available in English ranked third.

Why this matters for health outcomes

Research confirms that language barriers reduce access to preventive care, increase risk of miscommunication and diagnostic errors, and lower patient satisfaction and treatment adherence. Findings here align directly with that evidence base.

CAMI · Community Health Profile — Executive Summary05
Seven Cross-Cutting Themes

The patterns beneath the findings.

Synthesizing data across all survey groups, commissioned reports, census analysis, and partner interviews reveals a set of interrelated structural themes.

01

Mental health as a community-wide concern

Stress, anxiety, and isolation emerge in every survey group. Post-COVID social withdrawal has accelerated pre-existing trends across the life course.
02

Language as a structural health barrier

Between 23–40% of every age group delays or avoids care because services are not available in English. A documented access failure.
03

Social isolation across the lifespan

Not confined to seniors. Reported by adults, youth, school professionals, and institutions alike. 57% of adults socialise less than before COVID.
04

A service desert for the 15–45 cohort

Youth programs flagged as the top service gap. Adults 30–44 describe themselves as underserved. Without investment here, outmigration continues.
05

Aging in place without infrastructure

97% of seniors want to age at home; most have no plan. Transportation, home care, and volunteer services are fragile. An English-language seniors' residence is the top priority named by seniors themselves.
06

CAMI as essential bridge

Recognised across every stakeholder group as the organisation the community turns to for navigation, translation, outreach, and advocacy.
07

The paradox of economic strength and community fragility

The community is economically anchored by a successful fishery and tourism sector yet socially and demographically declining. High seasonal incomes do not translate into year-round stability, and provincial programs fail to address the realities of seasonal employment cycles.
CAMI · Community Health Profile — Executive Summary06
CAMI's Role

The essential bridge.

CAMI is recognised across every stakeholder group as the indispensable bridge between the English-speaking community and the broader health and social service system.

Rather than delivering parallel services, CAMI acts as a facilitator and connector — working with institutional, community, and provincial partners to adapt services, improve system navigation, increase awareness, and mobilize resources. The 2025 HSS Partners Survey found that 80% of health and social service partners said CAMI's involvement had increased access, awareness, and utilization.

Yet CAMI faces the same structural pressures as the community it serves: a small and aging volunteer base, limited staff capacity, funding uncertainty, and the weight of growing needs.

CAMI staff team, March 2026 — the organisation behind the bridge.
The Program Portfolio

Six programs, three populations, one community.

ITMAV

Outreach program identifying vulnerable older adults and connecting them with services.

SWI Grosse-Île

Social wellness programming, health education, exercise. Only program rated fully resourced.

Meals on Wheels

Home-delivered meals — food security, health, and social contact in one touchpoint.

Bright Beginnings

Early childhood program — language-rich activities for children aged 0–5.

YMHI Youth Club

Safe, non-judgemental space for youth 12–17. 11–12 of 13 youth attend regularly.

SWI Entry Island

Community activities and support for seniors where isolation is most acute.
CAMI · Community Health Profile — Executive Summary07
Strategic Priorities

Six directions for the path ahead.

Emerging from the convergence of survey findings, demographic analysis, literature review, and partner consultations. Organised by theme rather than by population group, reflecting the cross-cutting nature of the challenges identified.

01

Strengthen mental health & emotional wellbeing

Build on what is already working — YMHI, ITMAV, SWI — especially for youth and adults. Integrate mental health support into schools, cultural activities, and trusted community spaces.
02

Deepen social connection across ages

Flexible, drop-in, relationship-based activities. Intergenerational programming. Address transportation so people can actually get to activities.
03

Enable aging in place with dignity

Strengthen home care, transportation, maintenance, and volunteer visitor programs. Advocate for English-language long-term care. Help seniors plan proactively.
04

Improve language access & service navigation

A simple, maintained directory of English-capable services. Trusted navigation through community relationships. Translation of essential documents.
05

Invest in youth retention & opportunity

Build on YMHI. Address housing, skills, and employment pathways. Create spaces for connection over structured programming — as the youth themselves have asked for.
06

Sustain institutional capacity

Strengthen CAMI as the essential connector. Advocate for stable funding that reflects real workload. Collaborate rather than duplicate.
CAMI · Community Health Profile — Executive Summary08

Preserving our past, building our future.

This executive summary distils the findings of the full Community Health Profile of the English-Speaking Community of the Magdalen Islands (March 2026). The complete report — including detailed survey findings, demographic analysis, program evaluations, and methodology — is available on request.

The production of this document was made possible through financial support from the Community Health and Social Services Network (CHSSN), through the Networking and Partnership Initiative (NPI), funded by Health Canada. The views expressed are those of the author and do not necessarily reflect official positions of Health Canada or CHSSN.
